title: Memento Mori Decision Filter
html:
Memento Mori Decision Filter

Yahir's stable decision heuristic: when faced with a hard call, ask "if I were to die tomorrow, would I want to do this or that?" The mortality-frame collapses optionality into the option that matters.

Core formulation

From 2024 09 12 never forget do whatever: "When faced with a difficult decision think: if I were to die tomorrow would I want to do this or that." Not despair — a clarifier.

Mechanism

  • Decisions usually feel hard because both options have equal expected value under the assumption of unlimited time.
  • Imposing a one-day horizon strips away the slow, optionality-preserving move.
  • The remaining option is almost always the higher-variance, more-honest one.

Source summaries

  • 2019 08 15 voice memo 1852 — VC founder's mortality-clarifier line (speaker, not Yahir). Yahir recorded this; whether his own memento-mori framing derives from this talk or an independent source is open. NOT the earliest "audio expression" of his framing — the speaker is.
  • 2024 09 12 never forget do whatever — explicit articulation in Shanghai manifesto (Yahir-voice)
  • 2024 02 17 2162024 — Cam Jipp "die young" line

Where it surfaces

  • 2024 02 17 2162024 — captured Cam Jipp's "feels like he's going to die young" line. Same mortality-as-clarifier register.
  • 2024 09 12 never forget do whatever — explicit articulation in the Shanghai manifesto.
  • Echoed in the Dec 2023 rainbow framing (Sahil-Bloom-derived "your life is a rainbow").
  • Background to the departing China synthesis: the China chapter ended cleanly because each decision was filtered through "what would I regret not having done."

Companion frames

  • max effort commit — if you only have today, max-effort-commit becomes the only sane move.
  • fear of not doing — the dual: what dies-tomorrow surfaces is exactly the regret-of-omission.

Related

  • fear of not doing
  • soul as beacon
